title: Simnel cake
subtitle: Easter fruit cake with a marzipan layer and eleven apostle balls
excerpt: Simnel cake is a light fruit cake with a disc of marzipan baked into the centre, a second layer on top, and eleven marzipan balls representing the eleven faithful apostles. It is associated with Easter and Mothering Sunday.
sourceNotes: Simnel cake has been associated with the fourth Sunday of Lent (Mothering Sunday) and Easter since the medieval period in England. The marzipan layer and the eleven apostle balls are documented as the standard British form from the Victorian period. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932) — Simnel cake appears in the Easter chapter with the eleven-apostle decoration documented as traditional practice.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Fruit cakes improve markedly with time — make 1–2 weeks before Easter and store wrapped in baking paper in a tin. Apply the marzipan topping the day before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: The eleven marzipan spheres placed on top of a simnel cake represent the eleven faithful apostles of Jesus — eleven rather than twelve because Judas Iscariot is excluded. The balls are traditionally toasted briefly under a hot grill or with a kitchen torch until lightly browned before serving.

content.text: on top of a simnel cake are toasted until golden — do this with a kitchen torch or under a grill set to high, watching closely, as marzipan scorches quickly. The marzipan layer baked into the centre of the cake keeps moist even after the crumb has set around it.

Heat the oven to 150°C (130°C fan). Line a deep 20 cm round tin with a double layer of baking paper, extending 5 cm above the rim to protect the sides. Beat {{unsalted-butter}} and {{light-brown-sugar}} until fluffy. Beat in {{eggs}} one at a time. Fold in {{self-raising-flour}} and {{mixed-spice}}, then stir through {{sultanas}}, {{currants}}, {{mixed-peel}}, {{lemon}} zest, and {{whole-milk}}. Roll out 200 g of the {{marzipan}} to a circle just smaller than the tin. Spoon half the batter into the tin, lay the marzipan disc on top, then spoon over the remaining batter. Smooth the top. Bake for 85–90 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Leave to cool completely in the tin.

Roll out 300 g of the remaining {{marzipan}} to a circle the size of the top of the cake. Brush the cooled cake with warmed {{apricot-jam}} and press the marzipan circle on top. Crimp the edge with a fork or fingers. Roll the remaining marzipan into 11 equal balls and arrange around the edge. Press down gently. Toast under a hot grill or with a kitchen torch until the marzipan is golden and lightly blistered in places.
